speaker: interviewer
text: This is roll number three.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were telling us about how you came to United States.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah, that's an interesting experience. So that was already 1949. As I mentioned to you, today is the anniversary. I came November 30, which is exactly 46 years ago. Now it was not easy at all because they gave us a very hard time. The American authorities created a lot of-- a lot of difficulties for us for reasons I will never understand. One had to be in a certain year in a certain place in order to be eligible to come to this country. Eventually a friend of mine who was working for the employment of [INAUDIBLE], a Hungarian Jew by the name Neiman found his uncle in United States. I remember as if it happened today. His name was [? Levenger. ?] This uncle set up papers according to which he would commit himself to employ me once I came.

speaker: interviewer
text: The idea was that I shouldn't be a burden on the United States government, you know? And he promised that he will employ me the moment I come in.

speaker: interviewee
text: But of course, that was all really fictitious because the moment I came to United States, it is HIAS-- you've heard of HIAS organization-- Hebrew immigrant. It still exists, you know. This exists for, for many, many years. Took care of us immediately. They were feeding us and gave us a room in a hotel. I must tell you, it is a hotel which I shall never forget. I don't know if this hotel even still exists. It's on the Lower East Side around Second Avenue and Eighth Street, something like that. It's called Saint George, not to be confused with the famous Saint George Hotel in Brooklyn. This hotel and I was-- oh, I forgot to tell you that I was already with a child who was not quite two years old. Henry was born in Germany. Henry was born in Ulm, Alb-Donau. So he was about a year and a half. And they put us up in this hotel. It is the worst nightmare. There were rats there 24 hours a day running around in that room-- the size of a cat. And nobody had to-- nobody could sleep because my child was-- you know, my son was afraid. I was afraid. They were biting us. Unbelievable. Unbelievable. Huge rats, and that was the hotel that HIAS provided for us. They were feeding us all right. And I was there about three months, which was like 300 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And eventually, Terry found an aunt who help us a little bit getting an apartment. My first apartment was Second Avenue and Seventh Street in New York City. You know Manhattan? You know? Which was really like Taj Mahal compared to what I had. It was one of those renovated buildings. We even had an incinerator, believe it or not, which was really a luxury.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we still-- yeah, and then I-- eventually I found a job which was pretty miserable, but provided some bare, very bare, just literally bread and, and water. It was better than nothing. I was working for a factory that manufacturers fountain pens on Seventh-- somewhere around Eighth Street in Manhattan. And I was there for about three months. It was maybe $0.75 an hour because the law didn't allow to pay less. If the law would allow to pay $0.10 an hour, that guy would pay us $0.10 an hour, I'm sure. It was a typical perfect example of exploitation if one can get one. And I was about three months working on this job. And then I found another job which paid a little bit better, but it was an awful job. The job consisted of-- you know, it was a factory which manufactured brushes, painting brushes. And those brushes-- you know, a painting brush-- had to be shaved like to a certain size.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I would have to put this in a certain machine and cut them to a certain size. And I would come home full of hairs in my ears, in my eyes, in my nose, full of those hairs from those brushes. This was another like three, four months job. Then I had another crazy job in the factory that manufactured albums. Sounds like a very innocent job, but when I came home, it was not so innocent. It consisted of me putting pieces-- a piece of paper into a glue machine with one hand, and pulling it out with another. After I did that, one side of the-- of the piece of paper, the sheet of paper, was covered with glue. But if you do eight hours something like this, you come home, you still keep on doing it, you know? It reminded me very much of Charlie Chaplin's movie-- forgot the name. So what was your main occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my main occupation, if I can say so, was working for the bank. Eventually, I got through with all those miserable jobs, and I received a job in a bank, Citibank. Now it's called Citibank. At the time, when I joined that bank it was First National City Bank of New York. It's one of the oldest banks in New York. And there I was trained by somebody who was already working there for like 20 years in the area of letter of credit. I don't know how much you know or you don't about it, but it's a very hectic job, a lot of pressure, a lot of tensions. And with my luck, the guy who was training me was a Pole, a bloody anti-Semite. He didn't want to show me what I would call the tricks of the trade because he was afraid. You know, he knew I am Jewish, and he was afraid maybe I would get so good that I will steal some his secrets of-- you see, there is some-- to each job, there is some know-how, and it takes a while. It's not-- you cannot grasp this right away.

speaker: interviewer
text: But he, he deliberately didn't want me to learn this so quick so to make me more dependent on him. You know what I am talking about? But eventually I learned it, and eventually I was much better than he ever was, I think.

speaker: interviewee
text: But that job was, in a way, interesting, but it was very pressure-oriented-- I shouldn't say oriented-- but a pressure-involved job, a job which a lot of-- with a lot of tension, simply because you had to, all day, examine various documents. And on the basis of the examination, you would debit and credit various accounts, and we're talking about millions of dollars, literally. So if you failed, you are in trouble.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you end up staying then in New York?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I was in New York all the time since 1949 till I came to Florida.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then when did you come to Florida?

speaker: interviewee
text: Till I came to Florida? I am here about 16 years. So I was there like till '80.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you married. What is your spouse's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your wife's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife's name? Terry. But her original Jewish name is Tauba-- T-A-U-B-A, Tauba.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: My children, I have two sons. One is Henry, who was born in Germany, Ulm. As I said, he was about a year and a half when we came to the United States. And that's my oldest son. And my younger son is Isaac David. Actually his name is David Isaac. He changed it-- David Isaac. And he was born in America. He's a Yankee born in New York in 1958. And there's 11 years difference between the two.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did this experience change your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Which experience?

speaker: interviewer
text: The experience of going through the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a good question. The experience of going through the war convinced me more than ever, that it is very difficult to continue to have faith in humankind. And I always-- I never stopped admiring Anne Frank who said that in spite of everything, you know, mankind are good. Most of the people are good at heart. I still like to believe that, although it's becoming very difficult. However, I want to say that I'd never believed that there are real heroes. In my opinion-- my humble opinion is that it is all a combination of circumstances which made some people survive and some people perish. I know, for instance, as an absolute fact, if not for Russia-- if I were in German concentration camp, I wouldn't be around at all. Because I would never be capable of doing things that some people were capable of doing. I mean so far as-- you heard of kapos. Kapos were Jews who were working for the Germans, hoping that this will save their skin. And they perished anyway because the-- you know, the Germans didn't even plan to save them. They were only employing them as long as it was serving their purpose, which was destroying the Jewish people. The moment they didn't have any use for them, they killed them. Some of them survived, though. Some of them survived. But I would never be able to do that. My conscience would never be able-- would never allow me anything like that. However, I must say one thing. The most difficult thing to do is to be judgmental. And one shouldn't be judgmental because when your life is threatened, you would be surprised what a person is capable of doing to save your life. Even it is-- even if it is very much against your principles, it seems to me-- I cannot say because I was not in that circumstance. But I'm trying to put myself-- I'm trying to be fair, which is really the most difficult thing to do in life, is to be fair. I'm trying to put myself in the position of some of these people who were told-- whether they believed it or not, they probably did-- that unless they do this and this and this and that, no matter how low this is, their life will be saved. They believed in that, and they were able to do this. But I don't want to be judgmental because I feel that I have no right to do that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What made you decide to share your story with us now, at this time in your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Number one, I feel that my sons will benefit a lot by it. Although they know a lot about this already but, this particular occasion, it gives them a much better insight-- the combination of pictures. And whatever we said, I think. It's kind of a moral obligation, I feel, for us to do. And not only for my sons, but whoever will happen to look at this and listen to it may find this quite interesting. That could be probably said for anybody, for any one survivor, I imagine. But like I said, ours is sheer miracle, sheer miracle. My meeting my wife Terry is absolute miracle. 5,000 miles. Imagine that we didn't pay, or hardly paid any attention to each other-- being neighbors in the same building in Warsaw before 1939. And here we meet one another in 1942 or '43 deep in Russia, where huge country. And here we see each other, and here we are. It's totally unbelievable. It's really something for the movies.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you could say something-- a message of some sort to pass on to your children's children-- what would that be?

speaker: interviewee
text: That message would be never-- and I mean never-- to forget our experiences and never to lose hope. Because as hopeless as some situations may be-- that is maybe another factor that kept me alive. I somehow deep inside felt that just maybe, you know, maybe, some miracle will happen, and I will survive this war. That's one thing-- you just can't afford to lose hope because there's nothing else left. Once you do that, there's no point in living, really. So that is my message primarily to my children and to the-- to anybody else. And another thing that I would like to convey is not to feel that you are the most miserable or the most unhappy person in the world because you will always find somebody who is worse off than you are. Anything should be just in terms of comparison. That's what I'm trying to say. And never strive for the best or the most because there will be always somebody who is better off than you are. So try to be happy with what you have. That is in material sense, as little as it be-- see when I sit now and talk to you, and I have really very little by comparison with people-- by most of the people in this village are financially much better off than I am. I know it, and I don't envy anybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what I'm trying to say, I thank God for what we have considering all we went through in life. If you just have a little health, I think you are a multimillionaire because you cannot eat two dinners. I always feel that we all have to think in terms of comparison. There will be always somebody who is better off than you are. So what? Thank you so much for your testimony.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it is my pleasure, and, and I feel really honored by having the opportunity to say what I did because I realize that there are so many other hundreds of thousands who have so much to say also. And I'm very much trying not to lose faith in mankind.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you very much. This is my wife, Terry Her original name was Tauba, but in America she is Terry. And we are married for 50 years. And we seem to love each other.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's true. We have together a very understanding life, and just hope to happiness from children.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. The picture on the upper left is of my father in his times of being a student at Polytechnicum, which is a Polish equivalent of the MIT. And this is his official uniform. At that time, students wore uniforms.

speaker: interviewer
text: His first and last name please?

speaker: interviewee
text: First name is Izaac equivalent of Isaac in English. Last name, Laski, which is-- This is another picture of my father, Izaac Laski, in Warsaw, much, much later than the first picture. This is a picture of my mother, Rose. Her maiden name, [? Rochman. ?] Her married name Laski. And I don't know exactly when was this taken, maybe about-- well, I was born-- about 1918. She was about 20 or 21 years old here in Warsaw. This is myself. I was about six years old here in Warsaw. As I said before, Mieczyslaw is my original name or [? Mietek ?] Laski. This is a picture of my first cousin who's still alive, I'm happy to say. And her name is Beatrice Greenberg. And she is in New York, and she sometimes comes to Florida. And the little girl is her daughter who is in New York living now. And the person looking at them is their-- I mean, husband and the father, respectively. However, unfortunately he died a few years ago, Morris Greenberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in Paris. They were in France living for a while.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know when this was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Do I-- no, I don't. No, I don't.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you happen to have these photos?

speaker: interviewee
text: They send it to me from France.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had these from France?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who had them?

speaker: interviewee
text: My cousin. She sent them to me. My cousin who is now in New York. She used to live in France a couple years. Yeah. This is my Aunt Renia Mazuroff, which is her husband's name. And her husband passed away some years ago. She was living in Argentina for about 47 years in Buenos Aires and eventually migrated to Israel. And I don't even know for sure whether or not she's still alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sim or Sam. Sam, actually.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know where this was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. That's quite some years ago because she-- if she's still alive, she's much older than that-- much, much older.
